title: [MPS] Add `ctc_loss` backward pass
body: Stack from [ghstack](https://github.com/ezyang/ghstack/tree/0.14.0) (oldest at bottom):
* __->__ #188187

Closes #160828

The backward pass is broken up into two kernel calls, one to calculate `log_beta` and one to perform logsumexp-reduction of `
examples.target:
We need to skip the boundary row in the beta sweeps or we will end up overwriting the initialization for it for padded inputs where `input_length < max_input_length` as it gets recomputed from the row above it that was set to -inf in the previous iteration.

```suggestion
      threadgroup_barrier(mem_flags::mem_device);
      if (beta && t == input_length - 1) continue;
```

A minimal test case that'll exercise the problematic path:

```
  def test_ctc_loss_backward_varlen_mps(self):
      # Backward must match CPU when input lengths are padded (shorter than T).
      # T=20, batch=2, 7 classes; the second sequence is only 11 steps long.
      T, N, C = 20, 2, 7
      log_probs = torch.randn(T, N, C).log_softmax(2)
      targets = torch.randint(1, C, (N, 4))
      input_lengths = [20, 11]   # 11 < T is what exposes the bug
      target_lengths = [4, 3]

      def input_grad(device):
          lp = log_probs.to(device).detach().requires_grad_()
          torch.nn.functional.ctc_loss(
              lp, targets.to(device), input_lengths, target_lengths
          ).backward()
          return lp.grad

      self.assertEqual(input_grad("cpu"), input_grad("mps").cpu())
``` | Thanks for catching that! I added the fix and figured I might as well add the test as well

examples.target: `unzip()` writes zip entries to disk using `path.join(outputPath, entry.fileName)` without validating that the resulting path stays within `outputPath`. A crafted zip could use `../` segments (zip-slip) and overwrite files outside `.build/tmp-notice`, including repo files used later in the build. Add a path traversal guard before creating directories/files, and also handle write-stream errors explicitly so extraction failures reject the promise. | The feature-flag check is case-sensitive (`=== 'false'`). Azure Pipelines template expressions commonly produce `True`/`False`, and ad-hoc/manual runs may also set different casing. Making the check case-insensitive avoids coupling pipeline YAML to a specific string literal casing.

examples.input_preview:
title: [cudaMallocAsync] Trim the pool and retry once before raising OOM
body: When the `cudaMallocAsync` backend (`PYTORCH_ALLOC_CONF=backend:cudaMallocAsync`) returns OOM, the stream-ordered pool may still be holding freed-but-cached backing. Before declaring OOM, this change adds one last-ditch s
examples.target: The fix recovers by calling `cudaStreamSynchronize` on the very stream we stalled. So the instant B's first attempt OOMs, the main thread enters that sync and blocks there. It cannot flip the flag itself. A second thread flips the flag while the main thread is parked in the retry-sync, which ends the spin, lets the sync drain A's free, and lets the retry complete. The release is timed to land after B has been issued; releasing earlier would let A free in time for B to fit, and nothing would be tested. | Seems a bit scary, especially if there can be interference from the `per_process_memory_fraction` flag
